rationale: Yang and Lynn (2014) is a central target of the commentary, not a peripheral background citation. Simonson repeatedly discusses their 91-test replication evidence and explicitly argues that their stimuli and procedures often did not properly test asymmetric dominance. He contests their interpretation that weak replication implies the attraction effect is largely restricted to stylized settings, using their paper as a key opposing claim to rebut.
relation_description: The citing paper directly challenges Yang and Lynn's conclusions about the robustness and practical relevance of the attraction effect. It argues their replication designs often failed to establish perceived asymmetric dominance, so their negative replication results do not support broad claims against the effect.
